title: Mean Squared Error for Images
concept: MSE compares pixel values directly between input and output, penalizing large differences more heavily than small ones.

exercises.prompt: You compare two photos of the same cat, pixel by pixel. Which pair of pixels is 'more different'?

options.text: A pixel that's a little bit brighter
A pixel that's WAY brighter (way off)

exercises.explain: Bigger gaps between pixel values count as bigger mistakes — that's the whole idea behind MSE!

cards.title: How Do We Grade a Blurry Copy?
cards.body: Imagine an AI tries to rebuild a photo of your dog, but the copy looks a little fuzzy. How do we give it a 'wrongness score'? We need a fair way to measure how far off every single pixel is — that's exactly the job of Mean Squared Error (MSE).

cards.title: What is MSE for Images?
cards.body: Mean Squared Error (MSE) compares the original image to the AI's output, pixel by pixel. For each pixel, it finds the difference, squares it (multiplies it by itself), then averages all those squared differences into one single number. Small mistakes barely count, but big mistakes get punished hard.

cards.title: Darts on a Dartboard
cards.body: Think of MSE like scoring darts thrown at a target. Landing close to the bullseye barely costs you points. But landing far away costs you A LOT more than double the points — because the miss gets squared. MSE punishes wild misses way more than tiny slips.

cards.title: One Pixel Example
cards.body: Say the real pixel value is 100, and the AI's rebuilt pixel is 90. The difference is 10. Squared, that's 100. But if the AI's pixel was way off at 40, the difference is 60, and squared that's 3600 — a HUGE penalty compared to being just a little off!

cards.title: Myth: All Mistakes Count the Same
cards.body: Nope! A common myth is that being off by 2 pixels and being off by 20 pixels are '10 times as bad.' Actually, because MSE squares the difference, being off by 20 is 100 times worse than being off by 2, not just 10 times worse!

cards.title: Small Miss vs Big Miss
cards.body: MSE treats these two situations very differently, even though the pixel gap only changed a little.

left.text: Pixel off by 5 → squared error = 25 (tiny penalty)

right.text: Pixel off by 50 → squared error = 2500 (huge penalty!)

exercises.prompt: Why does MSE 'square' the pixel differences instead of just adding them normally?

options.text: To make the math look more complicated
To make big mistakes count much more than small ones
To turn all pixels the same color
To make the image smaller in size

exercises.explain: Squaring makes big errors blow up way more than small errors, so MSE really punishes large mistakes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In MSE, being off by 4 pixels is only twice as bad as being off by 2 pixels.
exercises.explain: False! Since MSE squares the difference, being off by 4 (16) is actually 4 times worse than being off by 2 (4), not just twice as bad.

exercises.sentence: MSE finds the difference between pixels, then ______ that difference before averaging it.
exercises.explain: MSE squares each pixel difference — this step makes bigger mistakes count way more heavily.

exercises.prompt: An AI-generated image looks almost perfect except for one tiny corner that is wildly wrong (totally different colors). What will MSE likely show?

options.text: A high error score, because that one wild corner gets squared and heavily punished
A score of zero, since most of the image matches
MSE can't be used on images with mistakes

exercises.explain: Even one big pixel mistake can spike the MSE score a lot, because squaring makes large errors dominate the average.

exercises.prompt: An AI recreates two versions of the same photo. Version A has many pixels that are each off by a small amount (like 3). Version B has just a few pixels that are off by a huge amount (like 60). Which one will likely have a HIGHER MSE score?

options.text: Version A, because many small errors always add up to more
Version B, because squaring huge differences creates massive penalties even from just a few pixels
They will always be exactly equal
MSE cannot compare two different images

exercises.explain: Because MSE squares each error, a few pixels being WAY off (like 60) can outweigh many pixels being just a little off (like 3), since 60² = 3600 is enormous compared to 3² = 9.
